coredata.dc:title: Study on visual multicolor intelligent detection of tetracycline antibiotics in various environmental samples by palygorskite-based fluorescent nano-probe

coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline is abused in animal husbandry due to the role of animal growth hormone, which causes serious pollution to the natural environment and is more likely to enter the human body through the food chain, thus causing harm to human health. Therefore, the detection of tetracycline is particularly important. At present, there are many problems in the detection of tetracycline, such as high cost, low sensitivity and narrow detection range. This research mainly introduces a new fluorescent nano-probe Atta-Eu-FITC based on palygorskite to realize the detection of tetracycline in environmental samples. The nano-probe has the advantages of high sensitivity (detection limit 19 nM), wide detection range (detection linear range 0–25 μM), high visualization and strong anti-interference ability. To improve the portability and practicability of the detection, a portable fluorescent test paper and a low background fluorescent agarose gel detection platform were prepared to realize the fast sensing of tetracycline. Combined with the smartphone chroma analysis application (App), the real-time and on-site detection of tetracycline can be realized. Furthermore, the fluorescent nano-probe can also be applied for TC sensing in practical complex samples with good anti-interference ability and accuracy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline is abused in animal husbandry due to the role of animal growth hormone, which causes serious pollution to the natural environment and is more likely to enter the human body through the food chain, thus causing harm to human health. Therefore, the detection of tetracycline is particularly important. At present, there are many problems in the detection of tetracycline, such as high cost, low sensitivity and narrow detection range. This research mainly introduces a new fluorescent nano-probe Atta-Eu-FITC based on palygorskite to realize the detection of tetracycline in environmental samples. The nano-probe has the advantages of high sensitivity (detection limit 19 nM), wide detection range (detection linear range 0–25 μM), high visualization and strong anti-interference ability. To improve the portability and practicability of the detection, a portable fluorescent test paper and a low background fluorescent agarose gel detection platform were prepared to realize the fast sensing of tetracycline. Combined with the smartphone chroma analysis application (App), the real-time and on-site detection of tetracycline can be realized. Furthermore, the fluorescent nano-probe can also be applied for TC sensing in practical complex samples with good anti-interference ability and accuracy.

Fluorescent palygorskite and rare earth integrated nano-probe displayed fast detection of tetracycline in a variety of practical complex samples
ga1

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their low cost and good inhibitory effect on many pathogens, including Gram-positive bacteria, negative bacteria, rickettsia and filter viruses, tetracycline antibiotics are widely used in the treatment of various infections [1]. Tetracycline antibiotics, sulfonamide antibiotics, β-lactam antibiotics and macrolide antibiotics are the most common in animal husbandry, accounting for more than 50% of veterinary drugs in South Korea and Denmark, and more than 90% in the EU and the UK [2]. In 2012, tetracycline accounted for about 33% of total antibiotic consumption in Europe [3]. In 2018, the proportion of TC used by German veterinarians was about 25% [4]. Residual and accumulation of TC in water, soil and animal foods after abuse poses a serious threat to human health and environmental safety [5–7]. Tetracycline not only affects the growth and development of the fetus [8], but also accumulates in the bone marrow, which is extremely harmful to infants and children. Many countries have clearly stipulated that the use of tetracycline antibiotics in pregnant women and children under the age of 16 is prohibited [9,10]. Therefore, the establishment of a rapid, sensitive and stable TCs detection method is of great significance for food safety and quality monitoring.

At present, high performance liquid chromatography [11,12], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [13], capillary electrophoresis [14], electrochemical analysis [15] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [16] have been used in the TCs sensing. However, these methods have the disadvantages of expensive instruments, complex instruments, complex sample preparation process, and the need for professional personnel, which limits their practical application [17–19]. Fortunately, there is a more popular detection method, namely fluorescence detection method, which has the advantages of low cost, simple, rapid response, strong sensitivity, specific recognition and visualization [20–22]. The ratio fluorescence analysis method monitored the change of fluorescence response of two fluorescence emission peaks with the change of target concentration at the same time, and the ratio was used for quantitative detection [23,24]. Compared with the single fluorescent probe analysis method the combined response of the two fluorescent probes significantly reduces the false positive risk in the detection process, and can significantly improve the detection sensitivity [23]. Attapulgite (Atta) is a silicate mineral with layered chain crystal structure, with unique fibrous crystal structure and developed internal and external specific surface area [25,26]. Attapulgite has the advantages of low cost, strong adsorption performance and no need to surface pretreatment [27]. In addition, its surface is rich in hydroxyl groups and has obvious negative electricity, which is easy to react with polar groups such as amino groups and becomes an important active site for grafting and modification, and is a carrier material with excellent performance [28].

In this research, a new fluorescent nano-sensing material based on Atta was constructed for the detection of TC, and the synthesis process was shown in Fig. 1. Firstly, the surface of Atta was amino-modified by APTES, and then the activated citric acid was grafted onto the amino-modified Atta surface. The rare earth europium ions were coordinated with the carboxyl groups on the citric acid. Finally, FITC was grafted onto the surface of the material as a fluorescent internal standard to obtain the multicolor Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe for tetracycline detection. When tetracycline was added, the continuous fluorescent color change of the sensing material from green to yellow, then to orange and finally to red can be observed under 365 nm UV light. At the same time, to improve the portability and instantaneity of the detection, the filter paper was immersed in the prepared Atta-Eu-FITC dispersion, which could be used after natural drying. The intelligent detection platform based on smartphone image processing technology can realize the real-time inspection of TC. A new detection sensor based on agarose gel was also prepared in this research to reduce the background interference of common filter paper on blue fluorescence. Due to the obvious color change of this fluorescent nano-probe from green to yellow and then to orange and finally to red, the semi-quantitative detection of TC was realized. Furthermore, the fluorescent nano-probe can also be applied for TC sensing in practical complex samples, which had good anti-interference ability and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Chemicals and materials
ce:section.ce:para: All reagents used in this experiment were purchased directly without further purification. Eu(NO3)3.6H2O (99.99％), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), anhydrous citric acid (cit) are purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ethanol were purchased from Kermel Analytical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide-hydrogen chloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Acros Organics. Tetracycline (TC) and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) were purchased from Xiya Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). oxytetracycline (OTC), thiamphenicol, kanamycin sulfate and chloramphenicol were purchased from Adamas-beta Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6 H2O) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Science Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization equipment
ce:section.ce:para: The micro-morphology of the as-prepared materials was investigated using a field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). The Fourier infrared spectroscopy were collected by a VERTEX 70 infrared spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Varian CARY Eclipse with slits width of 10 nm. The time-resolved fluorescence spectrum was investigated by Edinburgh FS5 equipped with a cooled R928P photomultiplier tube. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on Varian UV-Cary100 spectrophotometer. Ultrapure water was prepared by reverse osmosis methods (Aquapro, ABY-1002-U, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by ThermoFisher (ESCALAB 250Xi). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was performed by Rigaku-Dmax2400 X-ray diffractometer, and P-XRD was obtained in the range of 5–90° through a Cu-kα radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of Atta-Eu-FITC multicolor nano-probe
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, Atta (0.5 g) was treated with ultrasonic (1 h) and stirring (1 h) in toluene solution (50 mL), and then 1 mL APTES was added drop by drop to the above toluene solution. After stirring at 90 °C for 8 h, the solid was collected and then dried. The obtained sample was named Atta-NH2. Secondly, EDC and NHS activated citric acid (100 mg) were added to DMF solution (25 mL) containing 250 mg of Atta-NH2, and the reaction was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. The solid was collected by centrifugal separation and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C, and named as Atta-cit. Finally, 65 mg of Eu(NO3)3.6 H2O was dissolved in 15 mL of anhydrous ethanol and slowly added into 15 mL of absolute ethanol solution containing 125 mg Atta-cit. After reacting for 5 h under 60 °C, 10 mg FITC was dissolved in 5 mL absolute ethanol and added into the above reaction system. After stirring at room temperature for 10 h, the obtained Atta-Eu-FITC multicolor nano-probe was collected by centrifugation and washed three times with absolute ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The TC sensing performance of Atta-Eu-FITC multicolor nano-probe
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mg of Atta-Eu-FITC powder was ultrasonically dispersed in 2 mL of deionized water, and 100 μL of the nano-probe solution was added and dispersed in 1.9 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH = 9). The emission spectrum was observed by changing the excitation wavelength on the fluorescence meter to determine the optimal excitation. Under the optimal excitation, a certain concentration of tetracycline was added, and the change of fluorescence signal was recorded. The time when the maximum fluorescence signal value appeared was the best test interval. Under the best excitation and the best test interval, different concentrations of TC were added to record the change of fluorescence intensity. The linear relationship and CIE chromaticity changes were calculated and investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and anti-interference testing of TC by nano-fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of Atta-Eu-FITC to tetracycline antibiotics, including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), Kanamycin sulfate, Chloramphenicol and Thiamphenicol, was evaluated to evaluate the possibility of application in tetracycline detection. In addition, it was necessary to evaluate the anti-interference ability of Atta-Eu-FITC to common biological molecules and coexisting cations in the detection of tetracycline, including L-aspartic acid (Asp), L-histidine (His), lysine (Lys), glucose (Glu), K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Fe3+. Under the excitation of 365 nm, 5 μM of the above five tetracycline antibiotics, coexisting organic molecules or cations were added into the solution containing the same amount of fluorescent nano-probe, respectively. The ratio of emission spectrum I617/I517 was calculated and compared with the fluorescence signal of the detection system with tetracycline only.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Treatment of environmental samples and TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Milk, honey and fruits were purchased from local supermarkets and pre-treated according to the method reported in the literature [29]. After removing the proteins and fats in the samples, a series of standard concentration samples containing tetracycline were prepared. Water samples were taken from the surrounding environment and treated according to the method in the literature [30]. After removing the insoluble substances in the water samples, a series of TC standard samples with different concentrations were prepared. The detection of TC in environmental samples involved here was similar to the standard TC detection method mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of portable Atta-Eu-FITC fluorescent nano-probe and glove-based visual inspection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of paper-based TC visual fluorescence detection device: the common qualitative filter paper was cut into small pieces with a diameter of 1 cm, and soaked in 5 mL Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH = 9) containing 1 mg nano-probe. After 30 min, the paper-based TC sensor was removed and dried naturally at room temperature.

Preparation of agarose gelated TC visual fluorescence detection device: 250 mg of agarose was weighed and added into 10 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH = 9) and heated in an oil bath at 100 °C to dissolve, followed by the addition of 10 mg of Atta-Eu-FITC powder. After stirring for 3 h, the mixture was poured into the template and cooled to obtain agarose gelated TC sensor.

10 μL of TC standard solution (0–10 μM) was added to the prepared fluorescence sensing device. After 5 min, the fluorescence photo was taken by mobile phone under 365 nm UV lamp, and the color detection and fluorescence color analysis were carried out by an intelligent color recognition APP. The linear relationship between the fluorescence color change of the fluorescence sensor and the TC contents under naked eye observation was investigated, and the feasibility of the prepared tetracycline test paper and the agarose gelated fluorescence sensing platform for on-site and rapid determination of TC in the visual field was evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
The Structure and morphology characterization of Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology changes of nano-probes before and after loading fluorescent internal standard molecules and rare earth elements were investigated by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2a, the initial Atta was a nanorod-like structure with a smooth surface, a diameter of about 50 nm and a length of 100–300 nm. After surface modification, the surface finish of Atta decreased (as shown in Fig. 2b), and some tiny particles were attached. It can be seen from the EDS images shown in Fig. 2c and d that the characteristic elements S and rare earth Eu appeared in the nano-probe after surface modification, indicating that the fluorescent internal standard molecules and rare earth sensing units were successfully loaded on Atta. Furthermore, the Atta nanorods displayed green fluorescence after the modification of FITC, indicating the successful loading of the fluorescent dye molecules (Fig. S1).

The obtained materials were characterized by X-ray diffraction. As shown in Fig. 3a, the peaks at 8.50°, 13.74 °, 16.40 °, 19.88 °, 20.85 °, 21.45 °, 26.75 °, 27.50 °, 34.45 ° and 35.20 ° correspond to the crystal planes ( JCPDS No. 21–0958) at (110), (200), (130), (040), (121), (240), (231), (400) and (161) [31]. This indicated that the fluorescent material carrier Atta maintained its crystal structure throughout the whole experiment. In addition, Atta exhibited a strong peak at 26.7, which was the characteristic peak of quartz impurity in Atta structure [32]. The modification of Atta by APTES, citric acid, and FITC occurred only on the outer surface of the material, not on the inner pipe [33], indicating that the microstructure of nano-probes did not changed significantly after modifications. It can be seen from FT-IR spectrum Fig. 3b that Atta had strong Si-OH vibration at 3551 and 3414 cm−1. The peaks at 1030 and 791 cm−1 were due to vibration of Si-O-Si skeleton [34]. The new absorption peaks of Atta-NH-cit modified by citric acid at 1652 and 1433 cm−1 were CO and C-N stretching vibrations [35,36], respectively. The strong absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 after introduction of rare earth Eu was caused by coordination between nitrate and Eu3+ [37,38], which confirmed the successful coordination between Eu3+ and citric acid. In addition, in Atta-Eu-FITC spectra, the disappearance of FITC characteristic isothiocyanate stretching band at 2000–2050 cm−1 proved that FITC was successfully loaded on the surface of Atta [39–41].

To further study the surface composition of Atta-Eu-FITC, the material was characterized by XPS. It can be seen from XPS full-scan spectra in Fig. 4a that the nano-probe contains C1s, N1s, O1s and Eu3d peaks, corresponding to C, N, O and Eu, respectively. In the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 4b), the three peaks at 284.06, 286.19 and 287.6 eV were correspond to the C-C, C-O or C-N and CO chemical bonds of carbon element, respectively [42–44]. Fig. 4c indicated the three main peaks at 399.40, 401.45 and 406.67 eV can be attributed to the valence of N-H, N-C, -NO3- in nitrogen [45,46]. Fig. 4d indicated he three main peaks at 530.6, 531.45 and 532.50 eV were C = O, C-O and O-Si groups of oxygen elements [47,48]. The comparison of XPS full-spectrum data and FT-IR further proved the compatibility of Atta-Eu-FITC surface functional groups with elements, indicating that the fluorescence nano-probe was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Before the visual detection of TC by the prepared nano-probe, the multicolor detection mechanism of Atta-Eu-FITC was first determined. As shown in Fig. 5a, without the addition of TC, the fluorescent nano-probe only showed the characteristic emission peak of FITC at 517 nm, and there was no characteristic fluorescence peak of Eu3+. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that after the coordination of rare earth Eu ions with citric acid, a certain amount of coordinated water molecules around Eu ions quenched the fluorescence of Eu3+, so the probe displayed the green fluorescence of FITC at this time. After the addition of TC, as shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of FITC as the fluorescence internal standard was not affected, while the intensity of characteristic emission peaks of Eu ions at 593, 617, 652 and 695 nm gradually increased. The reasons for this phenomenon can be summarized as the following two points.

Firstly, the lowest triplet excited state energy level (T1, 19160 cm−1) of TC molecule with unique diketone structure was about 1860 cm−1 higher than the lowest excited state energy level (5D0, 17300 cm−1) of Eu ions [49,50]. When the electrons of TC transferred from excited state to Eu ion and return to ground state, the red characteristic emission peak of Eu appeared at 616 nm. Secondly, after the introduction of TC molecule, the coordination water molecules around rare earth Eu were replaced by TC, which greatly reduced the non-radiative fluorescence quenching effect of water molecules on rare earth Eu [51,52]. By investigating the fluorescence lifetime decay of Atta-Eu-FITC in H2O and D2O systems before and after adding TC, the number of water molecules replaced by TC can be determined. As shown in Fig. S2 and Table S1, the average decay life of rare earth Eu ions in H2O and D2O buffers can be determined by formulas (1) and (2) [53]. Finally, using Eq. (3) [54,55], it can be calculated that three molecules of the coordination water were replaced by 10 μM TC. Based on the above two factors, Atta-Eu-FITC can realize the visual multicolor fluorescence sensing of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline by Atta-Eu-FITC fluorescent nano-probe
ce:section.ce:para: Before TC detection, we investigated the optimal excitation wavelength and detection pH of the fluorescent nano-probe. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of the nano-probe was the strongest under the excitation of 365 nm, so the optimal excitation wavelength can be determined to be 365 nm. Then, the fluorescence ratios of Atta-Eu-FITC fluorescent nano-probe in Tris-HCl buffers with different pH were measured. 5 μM of TC was added into Tris-HCl buffer solution containing Atta-Eu-FITC at different pH, and the fluorescence ratios at 617 nm and 517 nm were investigated. Fig. S4 indicated that the ratio of I617/I517 gradually increased and reached the maximum at pH = 9. The ratio of I617/I517 decreased rapidly when the pH value was higher than 9, which may be due to the fact that low pH was not conducive to the energy transfer between the analytes and rare earth elements, while high pH was prone to precipitate rare earth oxides, resulting in a sharp decrease in fluorescence intensity. Therefore, Tris-HCl solution with pH = 9 was selected as the best buffer system for the determination of TC. Because of the similar structure of β-diketone in the rigid skeleton of tetracycline, it can quickly coordinate with europium on the fluorescence sensor and enhance the photoluminescence ability of rare earth Eu. As shown in Fig. 5a, with the increase of tetracycline concentrations from 0 to 25 μM, the green fluorescence peak of the fluorescent nano-probe at 517 nm was almost unchanged, while the red fluorescence peak at 617 nm increased. When the concentration increased to 25 μM, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum. Fig. 5b shows the relationship between tetracycline concentrations and fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I517), which proved that the fluorescence sensor had a good linear relationship with tetracycline concentrations in the concentration range of 50 nM-14 μM (R2 = 0.99645) and 14 nM-25 μM (R2 = 0.99576). According to the detection limit = 3σ / slope (σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample), the detection limit (LOD) can be determined as 19 nM, which was far lower than the maximum residue limit in milk (225 nM) stipulated by the EU. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of Atta-Eu-FITC to different TC dosages were calculated according to their emission spectrum (Table S2). As shown in Fig. 5c, with the increase of TC concentration from 0 μM to 10 μM, CIE coordinates showed a continuous red shift from green (0.2366, 0.3953) to red (0.5348, 0.3613). More importantly, the Atta-Eu-FITC ratio fluorescent probe can realize the visual sensing of tetracycline with the naked eye under the UV light of 365 nm. As shown in Fig. 5d, with the increase of tetracycline concentrations, the fluorescence color of the sample changed clearly, which provided a portable development for the next step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and anti-interference activities for TC sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the high selectivity of the prepared fluorescent nano-probe to tetracycline in practical application, we investigated the addition of metal ions, amino acid molecules and other drug molecules as interference substances into the solution of 2 mL fluorescent nano-probe under the same conditions [56]. The mixed system was balanced for 3 min and the fluorescence emission spectra were scanned. The interfering substances include L-homocysteine (Hcy), L-cysteine (Cys), L-lysine (Lys), L-aspartic acid (Asp), L-histidine (His), L-glucose (Glu), L-proline (Pro), Glutathione (GSH), K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 6a and c, the presence of the above interfering substances did not affect the detection performance of the nano-probe for tetracycline, which proved that the nano-probe had excellent anti-interference ability and potential for practical sample detection. In addition, compared with other antibiotics, the reaction of tetracycline antibiotics to this nano-probe was more obvious (Fig. 6b), indicating that the Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe had high selectivity for tetracycline. Although Atta-Eu-FITC had a certain degree of response to bother TC and OTC, the probe had a better response to TC, indicating that the Atta-Eu-FITC can realize the multicolor fluorescence detection of tetracycline antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable fluorescent visual sensing of TC
ce:section.ce:para: The above results showed that the ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy of Atta-Eu-FITC realized the visual TC detection, but this analysis method still needed to be carried out with the assistance of fluorescence spectrometer. In this research, ordinary filter paper was used to prepare paper-based fluorescent sensor for the on-site rapid detection of TC, which had various significant natural advantages [57,58]. Therefore, paper-based sensing materials were widely used in food safety, life science, environmental detection and other research fields. At the same time, the selection of paper was very important for the carrier of the fluorescent sensor. At present, the most widely used substrate was filter paper [59,60], which had the advantages of smooth surface, moderate liquid climbing speed, suitable pore size and low mechanical strength. Since most of filter papers were made of fibers, there were countless holes on the surface for liquid particles to pass, while solid particles cannot pass. Therefore, most solid ions were concentrated on the surface of filter paper and the pores of filter paper. Proper modification of the paper substrate enabled the filter paper to have signal response capability.

The above results indicated that there was a good linear relationship between the red-green fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I517) of the fluorescent nano-probe and the concentrations of TC, and the fluorescence color change could be obviously observed with the increase of tetracycline concentrations under UV lamp. Based on this, the Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe was dipped and loaded onto the common filter paper by a simple method to prepare a portable tetracycline detection test paper. As shown in Fig. 7a, when detecting different concentrations of tetracycline in environmental water, with the increase of tetracycline concentrations, the fluorescence of the test paper changed significantly. The color scanning application (App) downloaded from the smartphone was further used to scan the color changes of test paper [61–63]. As displayed in Fig. 7c, the red-green ratio was converted into the tetracycline concentration signal output, and a fast output of the chromaticity to the fluorescence intensity ratio to the concentration was constructed, thereby achieving the purpose of real-time tetracycline quantitative detection on site.

In addition, to completely eliminate the interference of background fluorescence, agarose without fluorescence under ultraviolet light was also used as the sensor carrier. As shown in the following Fig. 7b and d, the experimental results showed that the portable fluorescence sensor prepared by agarose had better qualitative and semi-quantitative detection effect on TC, and was more sensitive to color change and more visualized. With the change of tetracycline concentrations, the detection platform will show colorful fluorescence changes. Moreover, it can be seen that there was a wider detection range in agarose than in filter paper, which was of certain significance for the visual detection of tetracycline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid sensing of TC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To enable the nano-probe to be used for rapid visualization analysis of TC on the surfaces of common food, we prepared an economical and affordable glove-type portable wearable device. TC can be quickly visually detected by using rubber nitrile gloves that were easy to use in life, and a layer of agarose gel containing the as-prepared fluorescent Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe was fixed on its surface by using nano-glue. As shown in Fig. 8, the qualitative and semi-quantitative detection of tetracycline on the outer surface of four common foods was carried out by agarose gelated gloves. When the gloves contacted with the food surface without TC pollution, the fluorescent nano-probes fixed on the gloves showed green fluorescence. However, when there was a certain amount of TC on the outer surface of food, red fluorescence appeared on the fluorescent Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe fixed on the glove, indicating that this wearable nano-probe can realize the qualitative and semi-quantitative visual rapid detection of TC on the surface of food.

Since Atta-Eu-FITC had good detection performance for TC and excellent anti-interference ability, the following experiments used standard addition method to detect the TC content in four real samples. Table 1 illustrated that when the addition concentrations of TC were 0.5, 2 and 5 μM, respectively, the recovery rates of the above four samples were 95.20%− 105.87%, and the relative standard deviations (n = 3) were 1.53%− 4.78%, indicating that Atta-Eu-FITC fluorescent nano-probe had good reliability and accuracy for the detection of TC in actual samples. The performance of fluorescent sensors reported in the literature and Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe for TC detection was also compared. As shown in Table S3, compared with other TC fluorescent sensors, Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe had lower detection limit. In addition, it can also realize the detection and analysis of TC in many environmental samples and fruit samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, natural clay, common fluorescent internal standard molecules and rare earth complexes were organically combined to obtain a hybrid nano-probe that can rapidly and visually detect TC. The obtained Atta-Eu-FITC nano-probe had the advantages of high sensitivity (detection limit 19 nM), wide detection range (detection linear range 0–25 μM), high visualization and strong anti-interference ability. The nano-probe was loaded on the test paper and agarose gel to realize the naked-eye sensing of TC. In order to enhance the application of the nano-probe, a wearable smart glove-based nano-sensor was prepared to achieve the fast visual qualitative detection of TC residues on the surfaces of common fruit. In addition, with the help of color capture and color analysis applications in smart phones, rap id and intelligent detection of TC can be realized. The sensor also had a good detection rate for tetracycline in lake water, tap water, honey, milk and other samples, indicating that it had a certain application potential in the detection of TCs in environmental mixed samples.
